evidence: tts-playout-clock.ts release() begins with cancelRelease(contextId) (line 79), and cancelRelease() does clearTimeout(timer) + releaseTimers.delete(contextId) (lines 105-111). So EVERY manual release() path cancels the pending scheduleRelease timer for that context — the timer cannot fire after a manual release of the same contextId. All manual release callers go through release(): handleInterruptDetected (voice-agent-session.ts:929), handleTtsPlayoutProgress->noteProgress complete (line 71, called at voice-agent-session.ts:921), and the TTS stall watchdog (voice-agent-session-util.ts:149). The guard at line 58 'if (this.realPlayoutContexts.has(contextId)) return;' is a separate concern (estimate must not pre-empt a paced transport that owns release via noteProgress complete); it is not what protects against double-release. Most importantly, release() itself is pure idempotent state cleanup — cancelRelease + four .delete() calls (lines 78-84), emits no bus event and fires no callback — so even a redundant second call (e.g. timer fires when realPlayoutContexts unset, then noteProgress complete arrives later) is a harmless no-op with no observable side effect. The 'double-release' has no consequence and the 'fire after manual release' mechanism is closed by cancelRelease inside release.
real_fix: No fix needed. The cancelRelease() call at the top of release() (line 79) already prevents the timer from firing after a manual release, and release() is idempotent (pure Set/Map deletes, no emitted event or callback), so any redundant invocation is a harmless no-op. A generation counter would be dead complexity here. The lone real edge — timer fires with realPlayoutContexts unset, later noteProgress(complete) calls release() again — is observably equivalent to a single release and needs no guard.
